speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number seven This is Helen Jonas Rosenzweig. So your, your career-- you were-- some-- your husband encouraged you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I met someone who-- I definitely knew I had to help my husband, because it was getting a little bit difficult. We bought a new house, and I had-- we had twins, which I really didn't expect. It was a nice surprise.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, having three children and a new house, I felt I wanted to help my husband. I didn't know actually-- I, I couldn't go for nursing, because I didn't have the money, I didn't have the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somebody who I met-- a very special person, I must say, because she believed in me-- and she said, there is a beautiful profession that you would fit in so well, with your personality. You have the perseverance. You are the type that would do well. It was-- it was-- I was a little bit apprehensive because I felt going to school and leaving my children, they were so little. But my husband, Joe, had encouraged me. And he kept saying, you can do it, you can do it. I'll take care of the kids on his vacation. And I kept going. I, I was taking sessions morning, afternoon, and evening. I did it very condensed. And fortunately I decided I have to make it the right way. So I attended every possible seminar that was available in the field of electrolysis. And I became very active on the board of directors. And I really got into it on a full scale. And that made me rather good in the field, because I, I got a lot of information and knowledge, and it gave me a lot of self-confidence. And, fortunately enough, I was good at it. And, and, it gave me a wonderful feeling of importance, of, of of self-competence, helping people, at the same time earning nice money, which helped us. And life was a little bit easier. It was rather difficult, because I had three young children. But I was able to, to manage very well, because I liked what I was doing. And I made some people very, very happy. And, and I was good at it. And I worked for 28 years in the field. Very successful. I had a few dermatologists who always sent me new patients. And evidently, I had the good fortune of being good at it. And, and, you know, it was nice, because we could fix the-- our house very nicely. We lived very nicely. We-- you know, we enlarged the house, and and my children were able to go through, you know, college and educate themselves well. And we had a comfortable life in Long Island, in Wantagh. And then-- sudden death, you know, of my husband. And life goes on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what happened to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- it was a sudden death. It's-- you know, his heart. And he had high blood pressure. And it was just-- he was very young, and it was rather difficult to adjust to. But I have wonderful children. They're exceptional. They are just the most wonderful people, wonderful kids, one can ask for. I'm very proud of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel, Mrs. Jonas-Rosenzweig, that, that being a survivor helps you, when, when there are problems in your life, more, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that we appreciate the good things more than anybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it help you when your husband passed away? The fact that you had this other suffering?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a different kind of tragedy. The fact is that, when they came to me to talk about it, when the book was being written, the fact that I couldn't pull myself together to talk just shows you that it, it wasn't-- it wasn't easy at all. It was very-- But you know what helped me is my profession. I became so busy. I tried to fill in every minute of my day. Because that was like, like a gift from God. It kept me busy. And, and it was like a support system. And I had to be strong for my children, And they had-- probably did the same thing for me. We had each other. And memories are good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about what you went through during the Holocaust often?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, you know, it, it, it-- there are moments that-- there are scenes that bring back memories. Like, for instance, if I'm very, very cold-- like, if I'm, I'm really cold somewhere, and I don't have the right clothes on me, it just-- Auschwitz comes back to me at that moment. I don't like hearing trains. I mean, it's terrible. I mean, my father-- you know, my, my sister and brother-in-law just came from Poland, and they visited Belzec. And they explained that the Polish people were telling them what was going on. It's just terrible to think what that poor man went through. I have dreams.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you dream about? --and the dreams are-- Goeth is always there. Somehow, he's always there, and I'm running. I'm, I'm always lost someplace. And I, I always go the wrong way. And he's somehow always standing in a corner, you know? And that is, like, a-- I feel lost, always, when I get up. But you know what I do? I get up, and I try to think about something else. I start exercising. I, I start doing things, not to-- because that's the only way. There is no other way.

speaker: interviewee
text: You, you can't-- [BACKROUND NOISE] you can't allow the past to torture you like this. Though it never goes away. It really never goes away. You try to handle it the best way you-- I can. I really try. Because-- like, for instance, you asked me if I talk to my children much. I try not to, because I see their faces changing. You know, and I see the sadness. I see the pain on their faces. I mean, it's better not that they know--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, they don't have grandparents. They never know how to say Grandma, Grandpa, right? I'm-- [SIGHS] so-- I know they know. I know they know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now they know, because of all the articles. They heard me talking, and they're very proud. And, like, my children tell me, Mom, I'm so proud. You're so strong. I'm really not that strong. But, but, it's good that they think that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I know they'll be proud of you when they see your tape. I want to thank you so much for telling me your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to bring your husband out now, in a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad I could be of use to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you introduce the, the gentleman to your-- can you introduce the gentleman to your right?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Henry Rosenzweig.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my lovely wife, Helen Rosenzweig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, for the last few hours we've-- we've been speaking to your wife. And I thank you, sir, for coming on, on, to, to be the family, for the moment. Because your children are not around today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, I understand that you are also a survivor.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're from Kielce?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, from Krakow. From the same town with my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: From, from Krakow-- right.

speaker: interviewee
text: My first wife was from Kielce.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did the two of you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had some mutual friends that introduced us. And we took it from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand that you were with your wife at the premiere of

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only we were on the Schindler's list, we was on-- [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --VP invited to the dinner for Schindler, when Schindler's wife was there. And we were the first one to be invited there, because my wife was the, the lady from the she was the maid from uh-- what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amon Goeth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Amon Goeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think of the movie, knowing that your, your wife was a-- was a character of the movie? What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew in advance that she was, but the movie-- I only-- --the movie, I only can say, without the movie, nobody would know what's happened in the concentration camps, what's happened in Plaszow, and so on. This movie, I think Schindler should get any medals--

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean-- you mean Spielberg, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Spielberg. Did I say 'Schindler'? I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Schindler, according to my wife, he was this, he was this. But he have to get credit. He saved 1,100 to 1,200 Jews. This is the most important thing. Whatever he was. He wasn't a-- I mean, it's was a different character. Money, women, alcohol, and stuff like that. But he saved 1,100, 1,200 people. That's the most important thing. And especially my wife and her two sisters. That's-- I mean, it's unusual. My family is gone. There's not--

speaker: interviewer
text: --one person available from my family. How many in your family were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have one sister and two brothers. And, naturally, my mother. My father died earlier-- 1927-- in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when we called to ask your wife to be interviewed, did-- how did-- did you discuss it? Did you-- how did you feel about her being interviewed for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- she was already interviewed once and-- The Heritage Society. And then she told me she's going to be interviewed by Spielberg. And I think she's doing the right thing. Especially she have grandchildren-- I have only one daughter. She have grandchildren-- five grandchildren. And it's going to be in the history. It's going to be forever. And I hope that it's going to do the job what's supposed to be done, like never again.

speaker: interviewer
text: As, as survivors, do the two of you talk about your experiences during the war together a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: To some degree, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Occasionally we do.

speaker: interviewee
text: We discuss occasionally.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're going sometime to her friends. They are talking by her survival. I mean, she's going with me to my friends, they're talking by my survival. There's a few people who went together with me in the concentration camps. And, naturally--

speaker: interviewer
text: --any occasion, sometime they invited us. And so we're going. So. But it's not-- I personally don't like to talk about it. It's just memory, you know? I'm looking for to remember. I'd rather talk to my kids-- then my kids remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we are older people. We're old age already. I mean, it's not important. We're gonna to remember always. The important thing now, that's the kids going to remember, that they can do, you know-- carry on, and see what's happened, this never happen again. That's the most important thing. That's why my wife like to speak mostly where kids are. She don't like-- when she's speaking, she say, well, naturally, older women are, are not important right now, but the grandchildren are important, and the children are important. This is the most important thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I know that, that your family will be watching this tape, and I'm-- and I'm very happy that you came and-- came onto your wife's tape. And hopefully, you'll be able-- hopefully you'll change your mind, maybe, and be interviewed by the foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. Maybe. Maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well, I, I thank you, Mr. Rosenzweig.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a-- is there a last message-- I leave the last words to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I'm really grateful that, that I am still alive, and I am healthy, and I'm able to speak out. I never, I never thought I would never-- [BACKROUND NOISE] I never believed that I'd be able to speak in front of an audience. I mean, I speak to 500, 600, 700 people, in front of so many hundreds. And, and I am able to do that. It was hard, from the beginning. But I see how important it is because so many of my friends are dying. And it won't be long before there will be no one to be able to speak out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm really grateful that I am able and, um-- and be here, and for you people who are-- who are giving the time to listen to those sad stories, morbid stories. Because it's very difficult to comprehend something like that, for anybody who wasn't there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many times, when I speak in front of an audience, you know, I feel-- seeing people's tears, and I, I see they follow me. They're there with me. And, at a certain point, I feel they stop. I feel alone. You know, I don't know why I have this experience. But at a certain point, I feel like I'm alone because I, I don't think that, for people who were not there, can comprehend, can-- to, to really be there with me to the end. Only the people that were there could follow me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I feel almost like, toward the end, I'm all alone, unless I know there are some survivors in the audience. I'll be honest with you. That's how it feels. Because how in the world can anybody believe that something like that took place? That people could be so evil. What right do they have, to do it to one another? Well, now that you've, you've told your story today for the foundation, people will go to the museum, and they'll-- and they'll see your story, and they'll know it happened. And I'm-- I thank you so much for, once again, to-- for telling your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. We're glad to be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for you, Mr--

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to say a few words.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: That I am grateful that I'm in this country. And I think-- I've never think about it, that I'm going to be alive and be in this country. And I'm very successful still in this country. I work very hard. But no country give the opportunity to be successful comes here that [INAUDIBLE] and be so successful. And thanks God, and God bless America.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Thank you. Thank you both.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is my father, Szymon Sternlicht. The picture was taken in Krakow, Poland, where we lived. It was taken about 1935, 1936. And this is the actual picture that was blown out from the one that I have taken, in Auschwitz, into my mouth and saved, to be able to have this blown up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if this is the only photo of your father? Is this the only photo of your father? Because the picture of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the only one I have. That's the only picture I have. The one I actually tore around his face, from a picture that was taken in Krakow, most likely for a passport because this was the largest size of his face that I could actually be able to save. Other pictures were very tiny. And that's what I found, while looking through the rest of my pictures, before I threw it away on a pile of-- that where our clothes was, and we were supposed to throw everything away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother. And her name is Lola Sternlicht-- was, unfortunately. And she was born in Krakow. In 1930, 1931.

speaker: interviewer
text: This photo was taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken-- the photo was taken 1931, 1932. And this is the original blow-up from the-- from the little photo that I put in my mouth, in Auschwitz, to save her memory and see her face again. The more I look at her, the more I see that my sister looks like her. I have another picture of her that was given to us by our cousin, here in United States. And I'm grateful for the fact that I was able to do what I did, by saving those photos. This picture was taken on, on the Wawel. That's the famous castle in Krakow. On the left is my sister, Betty. Known now as Betty, she was [? Bronya. ?] On the left, with the pocketbook. And she lives in Florida, with her husband. In the middle is my, my-- this is Mother, that perished in the Holocaust. Her name is Lola Sternlicht. On the other side of her is my sister Sydell. She lives in Brooklyn. And, naturally, standing up is me. I was nine years old at the time. And the picture was taken in 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken? At that castle?

speaker: interviewee
text: On that castle. Wawel. That famous castle. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the castle--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the famous castle called Wawel. It was very known for its history. And there were daily people coming to visit that castle. I'm sure it's still there because I hear everybody who goes to Krakow goes to see Wawel. And it was very close to where we used to live. And I visited it quite frequently. This is Adam Sztab. He was my boyfriend in camp Plaszow. He was the one that was active in the resistance group. And he was very dear to my mother, at the time, in Plaszow. He was there by her side when she was sick, when she was ill. He loved her very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when did he give you this photo? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I had a lot of photos of him, and we had a lot of photos. I-- we had to throw it away. I just-- at that moment, when I was searching for my mom and dad, [SIGHS] he was on my mind, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I searched for his found, and I, I, I cut it around, as you can see, the face. See, that's the original size that I put in my mouth. That's the original size. I just lost my mother's and father's original, little cutouts. But this, somehow, I found yesterday. And I blew it up to a larger size.

speaker: interviewee
text: He, he was written up in this book. There are quite a few pages written about him, because he was really a very brave young man. He was killed at the age of 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that how old he was in that photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: 20 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, end of 1943 when he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my sister, Betty, and me. We were in Austria. This was in a DP camp, as you know-- a displaced-persons' camp. And we were free, at the time. And just thinking what to do, where to go, where to start a new life. But fortunately, I had my two sisters. I'm grateful for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're on the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister's on the right, and this is taken in Salzburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is-- I am on the left, and my sister is on the right. This is a picture of, uh, I have to say me and my deceased husband, Joseph Jonas. It was taken also in Salzburg. And we just started to get dressed up and be normal human being, I think. [LAUGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Those are only memories. That's it. That's us. Salzburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this photo? Who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those-- this is the three of us. The first one, to the left, is me, Helen. My sister, Sydell, in the middle and Betty on the right. And it was also taken in Salzburg, Austria. And we felt like dressing up, for a change.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 1945 or '46? This is 19-- this is already 19-- 194-- after 1945. Naturally, we were there for two years. So in 1946. This is a picture of Amon Goeth. He was taken to be hung, in Poland, by the Polish court. He was-- this was 1946. [SIGH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Just gives me the chills, looking, even-- seeing this face-- face of the size-- half of the size that he originally was. [SIGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah-- all I can tell you-- How did you get this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: A friend of mine gave it to me. I, I had few more, but I, I just don't know where they are. I had a few more of, of him. And this is the only one that I-- that was left. And that was him, going to his death. It wasn't enough. He should be tortured more. I always felt that it was too quick of a death for him. He should be tortured. But that's the truth. That was him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding picture. It was taken in 1947, in Salzburg. I was married with a borrowed gown, borrowed veil, [LAUGHS] borrowed flowers. And I was married by a very religious rabbi. I had to go seven times around the circle, and I had to go to the mikve. And there my friends made me a lovely party. And Rose and Michael Kroger gave me a beautiful present, a candle, silver candlestick, that I still use today. They were my very best friends. And I had my very best friends then. And those are memories. That's Ralph Fiennes, with me, around-- holding his hand around me. I was introduced to him by my friends, telling him that I am the actual one in the movie, though my name isn't there. But I'm the original maid of Amon Goeth. And he was very surprised to look at me, and I was surprised to look at him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he say anything to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked me if I am anything like him. And I said, well, you a little thin. He says, I gained a lot of weight for that movie. And I thought I would be as close. I said, but you did-- you were very good at it. And you portrayed him quite close to whatever he was. [SIGHS] No one could portray him, actually, but you, you did a great, great job.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was 1992?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1992. Yeah. This was the grand opening of the movie. And we were all invited to Essex House, in New York. This is me on the right, and this is Helen Hirsch. And the two of us were the original maids for Amon Goeth. The picture was taken in Israel, about nine years ago. And I visited since then again. This is my son, Steven Jonas, his wife Holly Jonas, and his two children. On the left, Jennifer Jonas, and Brian Jonas.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Shelly. She's one of the twins. She's a psychiatric nurse, with a master's degree. And I love her very much. This is my daughter Vivian, the twin of Shelly, and her husband, David Delman. She's a nutritionist, and he's a physician. And I love them very much. This is my grandson, Joshua Delman. He's eight years old, and I'm very proud of him. He's very, very smart, very bright, and very wonderful boy. And I love you, Josh. My beautiful granddaughter, Ariel. She's very, very sweet and very dear to me. Very close to me. She's very bright and drawing beautiful pictures. She's really talented, and I have great hopes for her. And I love her very much, and I'm very proud of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your other two grandchildren in the other photo, too, are--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I love-- oh, they are-- very brilliant, very bright, sweet. And I'm very proud of them. And I love you all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
